title: ✍️ Mock Code Questions (write-the-code)
blurb: Exam-format code questions in the professor's Mock-Q2 style: 'Write Python code to build/extend a model, compile, fit and evaluate.' No fill-in-the-blanks — write the full code yourself, then reveal the model answer. Covers MLP / CNN / RNN, BatchNorm, regularization, tuning, and classical ML (sklearn).

knowledge_points.title: Build & train models (Keras)
knowledge_points.recap: Mock-Q2-style: write full code to build/extend a model, compile, fit (with validation) and evaluate. Watch the output-activation/loss pairing and Flatten-before-Dense.

questions.q:
**Write Python (Keras) code.** You are given `X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val, X_test, y_test` for a **10-class** image dataset. Inputs are flattened vectors of length **784** and labels are **one-hot** encoded.
(a) Build a fully-connected classifier with at least two hidden layers.
(b) Compile it with an appropriate optimizer and loss.
(c) Fit it (don't forget validation).
(d) Evaluate test accuracy.
questions.answer:
```python
from tensorflow.keras import models, layers

model = models.Sequential()
model.add(layers.Dense(128, activation='relu', input_shape=(784,)))
model.add(layers.Dense(64, activation='relu'))
model.add(layers.Dense(10, activation='softmax'))      # 10 classes

model.compile(optimizer='adam',
              loss='categorical_crossentropy',          # one-hot labels
              metrics=['accuracy'])

model.fit(X_train, y_train, epochs=10, batch_size=32,
          validation_data=(X_val, y_val))

test_loss, test_acc = model.evaluate(X_test, y_test)
print('test acc:', test_acc)
```

**Marks:** softmax(10) output, **categorical_crossentropy** (one-hot), validation in fit, evaluate on test.
questions.extend: If labels were integers, use sparse_categorical_crossentropy.

questions.q:
**Write Python (Keras) code.** Given `X_train, y_train, X_test, y_test` for a **10-class** image dataset, inputs of shape **28×28×1** and **integer** labels.
(a) Build a small CNN (at least one Conv2D + pooling, then a Dense head).
(b) Compile it. (c) Fit for a few epochs. (d) Evaluate test accuracy.
State which output activation and loss you use and why.
questions.answer:
```python
from tensorflow.keras import models, layers

model = models.Sequential()
model.add(layers.Conv2D(32, (3,3), activation='relu', input_shape=(28,28,1)))
model.add(layers.MaxPooling2D((2,2)))
model.add(layers.Conv2D(64, (3,3), activation='relu'))
model.add(layers.MaxPooling2D((2,2)))
model.add(layers.Flatten())                     # 3D -> 1D before Dense
model.add(layers.Dense(64, activation='relu'))
model.add(layers.Dense(10, activation='softmax'))

model.compile(optimizer='adam',
              loss='sparse_categorical_crossentropy',   # integer labels
              metrics=['accuracy'])
model.fit(X_train, y_train, epochs=5, batch_size=64,
          validation_split=0.1)
test_loss, test_acc = model.evaluate(X_test, y_test)
print('test acc:', test_acc)
```

**Output = softmax(10)** (multi-class), **loss = sparse_categorical_crossentropy** because labels are **integers** (not one-hot). Flatten is required before Dense.

questions.q:
**Write Python (Keras) code.** You have built a classification CNN and saved two copies in `model1` and `model2`.
(a) Extend `model1` to match the output dimension using at least one more **Conv2D** (`num_filters` filters) and a fully connected layer, with appropriate hyperparameters.
(b) Do the same for `model2` but add at least a **BatchNormalization** layer and a fully connected layer.
(c) Compile and fit both (data in `X_train,y_train,X_val,y_val,X_test,y_test`).
(d) Evaluate and compare them, and explain why we split the data into training / validation / test sets.
questions.answer:
```python
from tensorflow.keras.layers import Conv2D, BatchNormalization, Flatten, Dense

# (a) extend model1
model1.add(Conv2D(num_filters, (3,3), activation='relu'))
model1.add(Flatten())
model1.add(Dense(64, activation='relu'))
model1.add(Dense(output_dim, activation='softmax'))

# (b) extend model2 with BatchNormalization
model2.add(BatchNormalization())
model2.add(Conv2D(num_filters, (3,3), activation='relu'))
model2.add(Flatten())
model2.add(Dense(64, activation='relu'))
model2.add(Dense(output_dim, activation='softmax'))

# (c) compile + fit both
for model in [model1, model2]:
    model.compile(optimizer='adam', loss='categorical_crossentropy', metrics=['accuracy'])
    model.fit(X_train, y_train, epochs=10, batch_size=32,
              validation_data=(X_val, y_val))

# (d) evaluate + compare
loss1, acc1 = model1.evaluate(X_test, y_test)
loss2, acc2 = model2.evaluate(X_test, y_test)
print('model1 better' if acc1 > acc2 else 'model2 better')
```

**(d) Why split:** **train** learns the weights; **validation** tunes hyperparameters / early stopping and detects overfitting; **test** is used only at the end to estimate true generalization and must not influence training or model selection (else the estimate is optimistically biased).
questions.extend: BatchNormalization() takes no arguments; never forget Flatten before Dense.

questions.q:
**Write Python (Keras) code.** Given `X_train,y_train,X_val,y_val,X_test,y_test` for a **binary** image classification task (cat vs dog), inputs of shape **64×64×3**, labels **0/1**.
(a) Build a CNN that includes at least one **BatchNormalization** layer.
(b) Compile and fit it. (c) Evaluate.
State the output activation, number of output units, and the loss, and why.
questions.answer:
```python
from tensorflow.keras.models import Sequential
from tensorflow.keras.layers import Conv2D, BatchNormalization, MaxPooling2D, Flatten, Dense

model = Sequential()
model.add(Conv2D(32, (3,3), activation='relu', input_shape=(64,64,3)))
model.add(BatchNormalization())
model.add(Conv2D(64, (3,3), activation='relu'))
model.add(MaxPooling2D((2,2)))
model.add(Flatten())
model.add(Dense(128, activation='relu'))
model.add(Dense(1, activation='sigmoid'))       # binary: 1 unit + sigmoid

model.compile(optimizer='adam', loss='binary_crossentropy', metrics=['accuracy'])
model.fit(X_train, y_train, epochs=10, batch_size=32,
          validation_data=(X_val, y_val))
loss, acc = model.evaluate(X_test, y_test)
print('test acc:', acc)
```

**Binary → output = sigmoid with 1 unit, loss = binary_crossentropy.** (2-class → sigmoid+binary; multi-class → softmax+categorical/sparse.)

questions.q:
**Write Python (Keras) code.** For an IMDB-style **binary sentiment** task, `x_train,y_train,x_val,y_val` are integer-encoded padded sequences (vocabulary size 10000).
(a) Build an **Embedding + LSTM** model.
(b) Compile and fit it.
questions.answer:
```python
from tensorflow.keras.models import Sequential
from tensorflow.keras.layers import Embedding, LSTM, Dense

max_features = 10000
model = Sequential()
model.add(Embedding(max_features, 32))          # word id -> 32-dim vector
model.add(LSTM(32))                             # or SimpleRNN(32)
model.add(Dense(1, activation='sigmoid'))       # binary sentiment

model.compile(optimizer='rmsprop', loss='binary_crossentropy', metrics=['acc'])
model.fit(x_train, y_train, epochs=10, batch_size=128,
          validation_data=(x_val, y_val))
```

**Marks:** Embedding first, LSTM, Dense(1,sigmoid)+binary_crossentropy. To stack recurrent layers, set `return_sequences=True` on all but the last.

knowledge_points.title: Improve & tune (regularization / search)
knowledge_points.recap: Add regularization to fight overfitting, or search hyperparameters with GridSearchCV.

questions.q: **Write Python (Keras) code.** A model is **overfitting**. Rebuild it with three regularization techniques — **L2 weight decay**, **Dropout**, and **EarlyStopping** — and fit it.
questions.answer:
```python
import tensorflow as tf
from tensorflow.keras import models, layers
from tensorflow.keras.callbacks import EarlyStopping

model = models.Sequential()
model.add(layers.Dense(64, activation='relu', input_shape=(input_dim,),
                       kernel_regularizer=tf.keras.regularizers.L2(0.01)))   # L2
model.add(layers.Dropout(0.5))                                               # Dropout
model.add(layers.Dense(num_classes, activation='softmax'))
model.compile(optimizer='adam', loss='categorical_crossentropy', metrics=['accuracy'])

es = EarlyStopping(monitor='val_loss', patience=3, restore_best_weights=True)  # EarlyStopping
model.fit(X_train, y_train, epochs=100,
          validation_data=(X_val, y_val), callbacks=[es])
```

**Anti-overfitting:** L2 (`kernel_regularizer`), Dropout, EarlyStopping (monitor='val_loss'). Could also reduce capacity or use data augmentation.

questions.q: **Write Python code.** Use **GridSearchCV** to tune the number of hidden neurons of a Keras model. Wrap the model so scikit-learn can search it.
questions.answer:
```python
from scikeras.wrappers import KerasClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV
from tensorflow.keras import models, layers

def create_model(neurons=50):
    m = models.Sequential()
    m.add(layers.Dense(neurons, activation='relu', input_shape=(784,)))
    m.add(layers.Dropout(0.2))
    m.add(layers.Dense(10, activation='softmax'))
    m.compile(loss='categorical_crossentropy', optimizer='adam', metrics=['accuracy'])
    return m

clf = KerasClassifier(build_fn=create_model, epochs=5, batch_size=32)
params = {'neurons': [50, 100, 250, 500]}
grid = GridSearchCV(estimator=clf, param_grid=params)
grid_result = grid.fit(X_train, y_train)
print('Best:', grid_result.best_score_, grid_result.best_params_)
```

**Marks:** `KerasClassifier(build_fn=...)` wraps the Keras model; `GridSearchCV(param_grid=...)` searches. Use `RandomizedSearchCV` to sample instead of exhaustively trying all combos.

knowledge_points.title: Classical ML & preprocessing (sklearn)
knowledge_points.recap: fit → predict pipelines with standardization, model selection, and data preparation.

questions.q:
**Write Python (scikit-learn) code.** For a KNN classification task with features `X` and labels `y`:
(a) split into train/test and **standardize** the features correctly.
(b) Train KNN and predict.
(c) Find the best value of **K** by trying K = 1..39 and comparing error rate.
questions.answer:
```python
import numpy as np
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsClassifier

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.30)

scaler = StandardScaler()
scaler.fit(X_train)                 # fit ONLY on train (no leakage)
X_train = scaler.transform(X_train)
X_test  = scaler.transform(X_test)

knn = KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=5)
knn.fit(X_train, y_train)           # 'training' = store the data (lazy learner)
pred = knn.predict(X_test)

error_rate = []
for i in range(1, 40):
    m = KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=i).fit(X_train, y_train)
    error_rate.append(np.mean(m.predict(X_test) != y_test))   # fraction misclassified
best_k = np.argmin(error_rate) + 1
```

**Marks:** StandardScaler fit on **train only**; KNN fit/predict; choose K by minimizing error (`np.mean(pred != y_test)` = error rate).

questions.q: **Write Python (scikit-learn) code.** (a) Train an **SVM** and tune its hyperparameters (`C`, `gamma`, `kernel`) with **GridSearchCV**. (b) Also train a **Random Forest** and print a classification report.
questions.answer:
```python
from sklearn.svm import SVC
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report

# (a) SVM + grid search
param_grid = {'C':[0.1,1,10,100], 'gamma':[1,0.1,0.01,0.001], 'kernel':['rbf','linear']}
grid = GridSearchCV(SVC(), param_grid, refit=True, verbose=1)
grid.fit(X_train, y_train)
print(grid.best_estimator_)
grid_pred = grid.predict(X_test)

# (b) Random Forest
rfc = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=600)
rfc.fit(X_train, y_train)
print(classification_report(y_test, rfc.predict(X_test)))
```

**Marks:** `GridSearchCV(SVC(), param_grid, refit=True)` (C controls soft-margin penalty, gamma the RBF width); RandomForest = many trees voting (`n_estimators`).

questions.q: **Write Python code.** Given raw data `X` (images 28×28) and integer labels `y`, prepare it for a Keras dense network: (a) train/test split, (b) flatten + normalize the images, (c) one-hot encode the labels. Then explain in one sentence why the data is split.
questions.answer:
```python
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from tensorflow.keras.utils import to_categorical

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.30)

# flatten 28x28 -> 784 and scale pixels 0-255 -> 0-1
X_train = X_train.reshape((-1, 28*28)).astype('float32') / 255
X_test  = X_test.reshape((-1, 28*28)).astype('float32') / 255

# integer labels -> one-hot (for categorical_crossentropy)
y_train = to_categorical(y_train)
y_test  = to_categorical(y_test)
```

**Why split:** so the model is **evaluated on data it never trained on**, giving an unbiased estimate of generalization (train learns, val tunes, test only estimates).
